learning_outcomes.description: Demonstrate knowledge of the broader historical, social and political contexts of Aboriginal and Torres Strait Islander communities, and the implications of these for their working relationships with you;
Demonstrate a knowledge of practical and theoretical issues underpinning work with Aboriginal and Torres Strait Islander people and communities;
Understand the types of discourses in which work with Aboriginal and Torres Strait Islander people is situated - both historically and today - and your own positioning to these;
Refine your skills in the self-directed acquisition and critical analysis of information relevant to a defined problem;
Demonstrate an ability to undertake independent research and share the results of that research with peers;
Enhance your capacity to articulate your results clearly, concisely and on time, in oral and written forms of the sorts required in the workplace and in higher degree research;
Demonstrate understanding of cultural safety in working with Aboriginal and Torres Strait Islander people.

assessments.task_description: You are required to write three storied reflections (300 words) in response to question prompts. Storying is the process of telling stories to communicate and reflect on information or knowledge. It involves writing about an event or experience creatively. This assessment encourages you to go beyond a description of a topic, theme or creative work, to critically reflect through story. Through the storied reflections you commence to weave your own ‘knowledge basket’ to critically reflect on what you are learning in relation to working with Aboriginal and Torres Strait Islander peoples. You will be give guidance to completing this task. This is graded pass/fail and requires you to write three storied reflections in response to prompts. All storied reflections must be completed by end of semester.
assessments.deferral_or_extension: You may be able to apply for an extension.
assessments.late_submission: A penalty of 10% of the maximum possible mark will be deducted per 24 hours from time submission is due for up to 7 days. After 7 days, you will receive a mark of 0.
assessments.additional_info: You may be able to apply for an extension.

assessments.task_description:
Across the semester, you will use a template to complete 6 short in-class written reflections, one in each seminar (weeks 2, 3, 4, 5, 6, 7). You will receive a different weekly prompt during each seminar. Your best 4 scores will be counted toward your final grade. The logbooks will ask you to reflect on what you learned in the seminar and/or in your own reading for that week's topic.

Purpose - These reflections help you:

·         Consolidate what you learned in that week’s seminar.

·         Demonstrate the changes or adjustments you made to your practice or thinking in working with Indigenous people

·         Build employability skills in concise writing and academic reflection

Format:

  • Handwritten in class
  • Template provided
  • Seminar prompt given on the day
  • You will have 30 minutes for each reflection
  • Approx. 200-300 words in total

 

Note:

  • Each weekly response is marked out of 10
  • 4 highest-scoring reflections contribute to the final 30%

Marking focuses on:

  • Insight and depth of thinking
  • Quality of analysis and reflection
  • Engagement with seminar content
  • Application of learning to your own writing
  • Completion of all parts of the weekly prompt
assessments.deferral_or_extension: You cannot defer or apply for an extension for this assessment.
assessments.late_submission: You will receive a mark of 0 if this assessment is submitted late.
assessments.additional_info: You cannot defer or apply for an extension for this assessment.

assessments.task_description:
You have been asked to design a qualitative research project exploring one of the topics listed below. Your project should involve interviews with key Indigenous people and be guided by the principles of the ‘R’s’: relationships, respect, responsibilities, roles, reciprocity, representation, and rights.

As part of this project, you will require ethics approval from The University of Queensland.

In responding to this task, you must:

  • Answer the ethics questions below in 1000 words, drawing on:
  • The UQ Indigenous Research Strategy
  • The NHMRC National Statement on Ethical Conduct in Human Research
  • The AIATSIS Code of Ethics for Aboriginal and Torres Strait Islander Research
  • Submit a 500-word reflection discussing the challenges you encountered when addressing these ethics questions.


Ethics questions:

  • What are the research aims?
  • What are the expected outcomes and benefits for participants?
  • What ethical considerations are associated with the project?
  • What would your consultation process involve?

 

Research Topics (choose one):

How does the UQ Art Museum foster collaboration between Indigenous and non-Indigenous practitioners, and how are these principles reflected in public artworks such as the Sun Stadium?


OR

How did collaboration between Indigenous and non-Indigenous people work in practice in relation to the ‘Stories You Wear’ exhibition at the Museum of Brisbane.

assessments.late_submission: A penalty of 10% of the maximum possible mark will be deducted per 24 hours from time submission is due for up to 7 days. After 7 days, you will receive a mark of 0.
assessments.additional_info: You may be able to apply for an extension.

assessments.task_description:
The final piece of assessment for this course is a 1500-2000 word critical essay. The topic for the essay is:

After completing ABTS3020 how will you apply what you've learnt within your chosen career? You are required to draw on your 'knowledge basket: storied reflections' as well as  policies and guidelines for working with Indigenous people in the career you have chosen.
